title: Economic Security and Technology
gist: This is a CSIS Economic Security and Technology Department landing page describing the department's mission to sustain US economic and technological advantages, with a roster of featured analyses on US-China tech competition, AI's effect on the middle class, trade-and-war dynamics, and CFIUS investment screening.

insights.claim: CSIS's Economic Security and Technology Department, led by president Navin Girishankar with over 40 resident staff, focuses on sustaining US economic and technological advantages across economic policy, global markets, and advanced technologies amid global threats.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Signals where a major bipartisan think tank concentrates its economic-security research, useful for tracking the US policy agenda on tech competition.
insights.quote: The Economic Security and Technology Department examines the most pressing issues facing the United States and its partners in sustaining economic and technological advantages essential to prosperity, security, and resilience.

insights.claim: A May 2026 CSIS Critical Questions piece by Lauryn Williams and Kuhu Badgi examines what the Trump-Xi summit revealed and left unsaid about US-China tech competition.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Flags that US-China tech competition remained a live policy theme around the Trump-Xi summit; watch the analysis for export-control and tech-supply signals.
insights.quote: What the Trump-Xi Summit Revealed, and Left Unsaid, About U.S.-China Tech Competition

insights.claim: A May 2026 CSIS commentary by Navin Girishankar argues for an AI Homestead Policy, framing the question of whether the AI economy will have a middle class.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Indicates policy attention to AI-driven labor displacement and redistribution, a theme that could shape future US tech regulation.
insights.quote: Will the AI Economy Have a Middle Class? The Case for an AI Homestead Policy

insights.claim: A May 2026 CSIS Critical Questions piece by Andrea Leonard Palazzi examines whether a Known Investor Program could make US CFIUS investment screening more efficient for attracting foreign investment.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Signals potential reform of US inbound-investment screening, relevant to cross-border M&A and FDI flows.
insights.quote: Foreign Investment Attraction and CFIUS: Can the “Known Investor Program” Make U.S. Investment Screening More Efficient?
